front: Film: first film screened at the White House; follows the Cameron and Stoneman families through the Civil War and Reconstruction; Lillian Gish plays Elsie Stoneman
back: The Birth of a Nation (D.W. Griffith)

front: Film: based on Thomas Dixon's The Clansman; widely credited with restarting the KKK
back: The Birth of a Nation (D.W. Griffith)

front: Film: 'The Ride of the Valkyries' plays during the climax as men race to save Lillian Gish from Silas Lynch
back: The Birth of a Nation (D.W. Griffith)

front: Film: Raoul Walsh played John Wilkes Booth
back: The Birth of a Nation (D.W. Griffith)

front: Film: four interwoven stories (Babylon, Judea, St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre, America) bridged by shots of Eternal Motherhood rocking a cradle; Theodore Huff called it a 'film fugue'
Film: Babylonian section — Griffith used a 100-foot crane to film women moving down a staircase below elephant-topped pillars; a king is delivered a white rose in a dove-pulled chariot
Film: American section — a peon of the 'Musketeer of the Slums' is sentenced to death with the recurring line 'an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth'
Film: ends with 'Dare we dream of a golden day when the bestial War shall rule no more'; made in response to criticism of Birth of a Nation
Film: in the most famous scene, Lucy spins in frantic horror hiding in a closet from her axe-wielding father who beats her to death
Director: Jean-Luc Godard paraphrased his quote that 'all you need to make a film is a girl and a gun'
Director: frequent actress was Lillian Gish ('First Lady of the Silent Screen'), who appeared in Birth of a Nation, Broken Blossoms, Way Down East, and Orphans of the Storm
Director: The Musketeers of Pig Alley (1912) is sometimes called the first gangster film; believed to be the first to use follow focus
